title: SEGMENT 11 — Making them human — from mouse to chimeric to humanized to fully human

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — Making them human — from mouse to chimeric to humanized to fully human
Phage display is one route to human antibodies.

The other route is what people did in parallel through the 1980s and 1990s, before phage display matured: they took mouse antibodies — coming out of the hybridoma machinery we discussed in Segment 9 — and gradually replaced the mouse parts of the molecule with human parts.
This is called humanization, and there are roughly four steps in the historical progression.
Why did this matter so much?
Because, as I mentioned before the break, when you inject a mouse antibody into a human, the human immune system recognizes it as foreign and attacks it.

That HAMA response — human anti-mouse antibody — can cause everything from mild infusion reactions to dangerous allergic responses, and importantly, it neutralizes the drug, so the second dose doesn't work as well as the first.
To make an antibody usable as a chronic medication, you need to make it look, to the human immune system, like a self protein.
That means stripping out as much mouse and adding as much human as possible, while preserving the actual binding specificity, which lives in those CDR loops we discussed in Segment 2.
So here's the progression.
Listen for the percentages.
Generation one: mouse monoclonals.
A hundred percent mouse protein.
Made by classical hybridoma.
Names end in -omab, the suffix indicating mouse origin.
Highly immunogenic in humans.
Useful for short-term applications — single-dose imaging, brief therapeutic windows — but you couldn't dose them repeatedly.
OKT3, the very first therapeutic monoclonal, was a mouse antibody and ran straight into HAMA problems.
Generation two: chimeric antibodies.
This was the first engineering trick.
You take the mouse Fab — the binding arms with the CDRs — and you splice them, at the genetic level, onto a human Fc region.
So the antibody is roughly two-thirds human (the Fc and the framework regions of the constant domains) and one-third mouse (the variable regions where the binding happens).
Less immunogenic than pure mouse, but still meaningfully foreign.
Names end in -ximab, with the "xi" hinting at chimeric.
Rituximab is the canonical example, and we'll meet it in the next segment.
Generation three: humanized antibodies.
This was the breakthrough that took most of the 1990s to perfect.
The idea was to go further — to keep only the absolutely essential mouse parts and replace everything else with human.
The "essential" parts turn out to be just the CDR loops themselves — those six little fingertip loops we talked about in Segment 2 that actually contact the antigen.
So in a humanized antibody, you take the human framework — almost the entire antibody — and you graft, surgically at the genetic level, the six mouse CDR loops onto it.
The molecule is now roughly 90-95% human, with only the tiny CDR loops being of mouse origin.
This is called CDR grafting.

The catch is that the CDRs don't always fit perfectly onto a foreign framework — sometimes you need to also transfer a few neighboring framework amino acids to keep the CDRs in the right shape — and that fiddly engineering took years to figure out.
Names end in -zumab.
Trastuzumab is the canonical example — Herceptin, against breast cancer.
Generation four: fully human antibodies.
Zero mouse.

Made either by phage display (as we discussed in Segment 10) or by a beautiful alternative trick called transgenic mice: scientists engineered mice in which the mouse antibody genes had been knocked out and replaced with human antibody genes.

Immunize one of these mice, run hybridoma against its B cells, and the resulting antibody is fully human — a human antibody that just happens to have been generated inside a mouse, by a mouse's immune system using human gene segments.
Names end in -umab.
Adalimumab (Humira) is the famous example.
So now you have a vocabulary trick for reading drug labels.
Look at the last few letters of any monoclonal antibody name and you can immediately tell its origin.
-omab: pure mouse.
-ximab: chimeric.
-zumab: humanized.
-umab: fully human.
Rituximab is chimeric.
Trastuzumab is humanized.
Adalimumab is fully human.
The naming wasn't accidental; it was designed by a committee specifically to encode this information.
And the underlying lesson is one about the immunogenicity gradient.
The more human the antibody, the better tolerated it is for repeat dosing — which is essential for chronic diseases like autoimmunity, where patients need monthly injections for years.
The progression from mouse to chimeric to humanized to fully human is essentially a story about getting therapeutic antibodies to a place where they can be used as long-term medications.
Once we got there, in roughly the late 1990s and 2000s, the floodgates opened.
Almost every modern antibody drug is humanized or fully human, and that's why they can be dosed indefinitely without the immune system rejecting them.
